Article Name: The world wants Chinese tech. China is determined to keep it
Article Text:
THEY USED to gripe about too much technology transfer in China. But in the past year or so, foreign business and political leaders have started to fret that too little is happening. No longer do they worry so much about Western tech landing in Chinese hands; rather, they fear that China is now too effective at preventing its best stuff from passing to foreigners. A former Chinese trade official reacts to the pivot with empathy rather than mockery. “It is a bit hypocritical but it’s understandable,” he says.

It might be tempting to craft a morality play out of this, as if countries outside China are getting their comeuppance. But at its heart this is a practical problem, a question of whether China will be able to dig a moat around its world-leading technologies, from electric vehicles to artificial-intelligence-powered robots. Chaguan is inclined to take the other side of the bet—namely, that knowledge will flow as it normally does, from those who have it to those who want it. A reverse tech transfer will, over time, occur.

In principle, the mechanism is straightforward. Countries can offer Chinese firms market access as long as they set up local manufacturing. In practice, none of this is automatic—and all of it is fraught. The European Union is now at the forefront, recently proposing procurement rules that would require things such as battery-storage systems for Europe to be made there. Chinese companies wanting to be let in to European markets would have to invest in factories there. Developing countries also see promise. From Brazil to Vietnam, governments are opening their doors to Chinese EV companies and urging them to use local content. Yet it is early days. “We have been talking about tech transfer for just the past year and it’s still not really clear how it will work,” says one diplomat with refreshing candour.

One obstacle will be China itself. Over the past five years it has built an export-control regime, mimicking America’s. The stated goal is to protect national security but many controls are aimed at shoring up Chinese industry. Last year, for example, the commerce ministry said it would require companies to obtain licences before exporting technologies used in EV batteries. A Western trade official sees little prospect of Chinese officials letting out anything genuinely valuable. They have already reacted angrily to the made-in-Europe legislation, viewing it as a ploy to weaken Chinese industry. The saga of Manus, an AI startup, also highlights Chinese leaders’ paranoia. Founded in China, Manus shifted its business registration to Singapore last year, facilitating a sale to Meta, Facebook’s parent company. Chinese regulators are now attempting to unwind that deal and have barred Manus’s two co-founders from leaving the country.

But China’s own record in digesting foreign tech offers some guidance for the rest of the world. Persistence will be essential. China took three decades to hone its methods: incentives for investors; requirements for local joint ventures; content rules; partnerships with foreign universities; and, yes, theft of intellectual property. One critical need is more attention to Chinese scientific research: much of it, at least in pre-commercial phases, is in journals, says Tai Ming Cheung, an analyst of Chinese tech policy. “It’s something we didn’t think about that now we have to,” he says.

Moreover, it is a mistake to think tech transfer involves discrete bits of technology, such as a blueprint for photovoltaic cells. Instead, what matters is the whole process, including workforce training—a lesson that India is slowly absorbing. Apple, whose investments helped build up China’s supply chains, now produces a quarter of its iPhones in India. Even if most components are sourced from China, many are made in Chinese factories owned by foreign companies, according to analysis by Chris Miller and Vishnu Venugopalan for the American Enterprise Institute, a think-tank. The implication is that the Chinese supply chain may ultimately prove more mobile than it currently seems.

At the same time the rest of the world will have to follow a different formula from China’s. Instead of relying on the government’s top-down strategy, other countries will have to depend on companies themselves. The auto sector offers an early indication. Almost all global carmakers—General Motors, Hyundai and Volkswagen, to name a few—are now developing EVs in China, learning from local firms. Many ideas are also seeping out through research-and-development partnerships. “The biggest companies are off limits but there are thousands of suppliers to work with,” says one Chinese executive with a foreign carmaker.

China’s tech controls may also have the opposite of their intended effect. For young innovators in AI, the crackdown on Manus is chilling. If they are unable to sell to foreigners, they will never get full value. That will only give them more incentive to take ideas abroad early on. The ultra-competitive Chinese business environment pushes in the same direction. The Chinese auto executive says many local suppliers are starved for profits, which makes foreign partnerships yet more tempting.

For all its manufacturing prowess, China still wants what the West has to offer, notably in the semiconductor industry. In that sense the current wrangling over tech transfers has an air of negotiation to it. As the former Chinese trade official puts it, China will not fully benefit from its inventiveness unless it can export its own technology. The point, he says, is to extract a price from others, including access to their latest innovations.

This is not a winning proposition for now, especially as American officials want to keep China away from cutting-edge chips. But it points to a possible, even a likely, future in which tech flows both ways across China’s borders. That may worry leaders in both Beijing and Washington. They will, however, struggle to stop it.
GPT Summary: The article "The world wants Chinese tech. China is determined to keep it" examines the growing global demand for Chinese technology and the complications surrounding its transfer. Initially, there was concern about Western technologies getting into Chinese hands, but now the worry is that China is effectively preventing its advanced technologies from reaching foreigners. This situation presents a practical challenge, questioning whether China can fully safeguard its leading technologies like electric vehicles and AI-powered robots from spreading globally. Some countries are attempting to encourage tech transfer by offering market access to Chinese firms in exchange for local manufacturing, but China's export controls and efforts to protect its industries pose significant barriers. China's past experiences with foreign tech indicate persistence is key, involving practices like joint ventures and partnerships, and even intellectual property theft. However, unlike China’s centralized approach, other countries may need to rely on private company initiatives for tech transfer. The article suggests that despite China’s stringent controls, technology will likely flow both ways in the future, driven by commercial interests and global interdependencies, although this is not currently a reality, especially with the semiconductor industry and geopolitical tensions.

Other countries are attempting to incentivize this by offering market access in exchange for local manufacturing and are learning from China's own past strategies of persistent, holistic tech absorption. Moreover, China's restrictive policies and ultra-competitive domestic market may inadvertently push its innovators and suppliers to seek foreign partnerships and markets, making a two-way exchange of technology across borders likely, despite attempts by governments to control it.

Ultimately, because China still needs Western technology, particularly in semiconductors, a messy, two-way flow of innovation is likely to emerge despite the desires of both Beijing and Washington to control it.

Article Name: Google Releases Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1 for Pixel
Article Text:
Google surprised us a bit this afternoon with the release of Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1 for its Pixel line-up. This update will come after the first stable build of Android 17 is released, which should happen soon now that we have received the final beta of that.

For those not up-to-date on what the QPR program is, here’s a little refreshed. Years ago, Google started releasing Quarterly Platform Release (QPR) builds in beta form for Pixel devices to test. These updates come out quarterly and often include dozens of bug fixes as well as some new features. They often also release alongside a Pixel Feature Drop. They release as opportunities for Pixel owners to test bigger updates and give Google feedback before they go stable to the public.

To see the first Android 17 QPR1 Beta already seems a bit odd, but we’ll take it. Google has already said that this build will go stable as a part of the September Feature Drop, so we’ll be testing it for months.

The first Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1 is here as build CP31.260403.005.A1 for all still-supported Pixel devices. The list of devices includes the Pixel 6, 6 Pro, 6a, 7, 7 Pro, 7a, 8, 8 Pro, 8a, 9, 9 Pro, 9a, Pixel 10, 10 Pro, 10 Pro XL, 10 Pro Fold, 10a, Pixel Tablet series devices.

As for bug fixes, Google supplied the following list:

Fixed a crash in the Default Print Service occurring during low ink conditions that prevents users from completing print jobs. (Issue #487545419)
The Terminal app triggers an Application Not Responding (ANR) error that results in the application and device becoming unresponsive. (Issue #497465940)
Resolved an issue where uncontrollable hardware audio processing on the voice communication path caused distortion and phase cancellation in VoIP applications. (Issue #494843726)
Direct audio output may fail to open on devices using the AIDL audio HAL when playing audio streams longer than five seconds. (Issue #372064012)
How do you get this Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1? If you are a part of the Android Beta Program (sign-up) you can download this update right now. Even if you were just updated to Android 17 Beta 4, this update is available to you – my Pixel 10 Pro just grabbed it.

We’ll let you know if we find anything fun.
GPT Summary: Google has released the Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1 for its Pixel devices, following the final beta of the initial Android 17 stable build, expected to launch soon. The Quarterly Platform Release (QPR) program allows for beta testing of updates on Pixel devices, delivering new features and numerous bug fixes, often alongside Pixel Feature Drops. Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1, identified as build CP31.260403.005.A1, is available for Pixel models from the 6 to the 10 series, including the Pixel Tablet series. This update addresses several issues, such as crashes in the Default Print Service during low ink conditions, ANR errors from the Terminal app, hardware audio processing issues in VoIP applications, and issues with direct audio output on certain devices. Those enrolled in the Android Beta Program can download the update immediately, even if they have recently installed Android 17 Beta 4.
Gemini Summary: Google has unexpectedly released Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1 for its Pixel lineup, an update meant to follow the stable Android 17 release. Quarterly Platform Releases (QPRs) are beta builds for Pixel devices that offer bug fixes and new features, often coinciding with Pixel Feature Drops, allowing users to test major updates and provide feedback. Although its early arrival is noted, this specific QPR1 Beta 1 (build CP31.260403.005.A1) is slated to become stable with the September Feature Drop. It's available for all supported Pixel devices, ranging from the Pixel 6 series up to the Pixel 10 series and Pixel Tablet devices, and includes fixes for several issues like printing crashes and audio distortion. Users enrolled in the Android Beta Program can download the update now.
Grok Summary:
**Summary:**

Google has released Android 17 QPR1 Beta 1 for all currently supported Pixel devices, including the Pixel 6 through 10 series, Pixel Tablets, and various Pro, a, and Fold models. The update (build CP31.260403.005.A1) arrives shortly after the final Android 17 beta and will eventually become stable as part of the September Feature Drop. As a Quarterly Platform Release (QPR), it focuses primarily on bug fixes and stability improvements, addressing issues such as crashes in the Default Print Service, Terminal app ANRs, audio distortion in VoIP calls, and problems with direct audio output on AIDL HAL devices. The beta is immediately available to all users enrolled in the Android Beta Program.

Article Name: Massive Motorola Razr 2026 leak leaves nothing to the imagination
Article Text:
A new leak has spilled almost everything we need to know about the Motorola Razr 2026 ahead of its April 29 launch. After weeks of leaks, the standard Razr 2026 has now fully surfaced via a Russian retailer listing, detailing all the upgrades Moto is making over last year’s model.

The listing reveals the Razr 2026 in gray, green, and white colors, though there might also be a fourth pink variant in the lineup. Design-wise, there are no surprises. We’ve already seen what the Razr 2026 looks like thanks to previous leaks, but the specs are where the real story lies.

Up front, you’re looking at a 6.9-inch LTPO AMOLED inner display with a 120Hz refresh rate and 2640 x 1080 resolution. The cover screen remains a 3.6-inch panel with a 90Hz refresh rate and up to 1,700 nits peak brightness. That’s very much in line with last year’s setup, which isn’t a bad thing given Motorola already had one of the more functional outer displays in this category.

One of the biggest changes comes under the hood. The Razr 2026 is powered by the MediaTek Dimensity 7450X, paired with 8GB of LPDDR5X RAM and 256GB of UFS 3.1 storage.

While this still isn’t flagship-tier silicon, it should deliver better performance compared to last year.

If there’s one area where the Razr 2026 makes a notable leap, it’s the battery life. The device is listed with a 4,800mAh battery, which is a solid bump over the Razr 2025’s 4,500mAh battery. It might not look like much, but every little jump matters in a flip phone. Battery life has traditionally been one of the biggest compromises in this form factor, so this is definitely a welcome increase.

Meanwhile, charging specs remain unchanged, including 30W wired and 15W wireless charging.

On the camera front, Motorola appears to be going with a 50MP + 50MP dual rear setup, along with a 32MP front-facing camera.

The phone is also rated IP48, offering some level of dust and water resistance.

The Razr 2026 should run Android 16 with Motorola’s Hello UI and include features like Bluetooth 5.4, NFC, and support for both SIM and eSIM.

As for pricing, a very recent leak suggests the Razr 2026 could cost $799.99, up $100 from last year’s model.
GPT Summary: A comprehensive leak about the Motorola Razr 2026 has unveiled nearly all details ahead of its launch on April 29. A listing from a Russian retailer showcases upgrades from last year’s model and reveals the phone's availability in gray, green, white, and potentially pink. The Razr 2026 maintains a familiar design but enhances its internal features, with a 6.9-inch LTPO AMOLED inner display boasting a 120Hz refresh rate, paired with a 3.6-inch outer screen with a 90Hz refresh rate. One significant improvement is the switch to a MediaTek Dimensity 7450X processor, complemented by 8GB RAM and 256GB storage, promising better performance. The phone also sees a boost in battery capacity with a 4,800mAh battery, up from 4,500mAh, but retains the same 30W wired and 15W wireless charging capabilities. It comes with a dual 50MP rear camera setup and a 32MP front camera, rated IP48 for dust and water resistance. The device runs Android 16 with Motorola’s Hello UI, featuring Bluetooth 5.4, NFC, and support for SIM and eSIM. The Razr 2026 is expected to be priced at $799.99, a $100 increase from its predecessor.
Gemini Summary: A recent leak from a Russian retailer has revealed almost all details about the Motorola Razr 2026 ahead of its April 29 launch. The new foldable will come in gray, green, and white, featuring largely similar displays to its predecessor: a 6.9-inch 120Hz inner screen and a 3.6-inch 90Hz cover screen. Key upgrades include a MediaTek Dimensity 7450X processor paired with 8GB RAM and 256GB storage, and a notable bump in battery capacity to 4,800mAh, addressing a common flip phone limitation. Other specifications include a dual 50MP rear camera setup, a 32MP front camera, an IP48 rating for dust and water resistance, Android 16 with Hello UI, and Bluetooth 5.4. While charging speeds remain at 30W wired and 15W wireless, the Razr 2026 is expected to see a price increase, potentially costing $799.99.
Grok Summary:
**Summary:**

A comprehensive leak from a Russian retailer has revealed nearly all the details of the upcoming Motorola Razr 2026, ahead of its scheduled April 29 launch. The foldable will feature a 6.9-inch 120Hz LTPO AMOLED inner display and a 3.6-inch 90Hz cover screen, powered by the MediaTek Dimensity 7450X chipset with 8GB of RAM and 256GB of storage. The biggest upgrade is the larger 4,800mAh battery (up from 4,500mAh), while charging speeds remain at 30W wired and 15W wireless. It also includes a 50MP dual rear camera system, a 32MP selfie camera, IP48 rating, Android 16 with Motorola’s Hello UI, and connectivity features like Bluetooth 5.4 and NFC. The phone will be available in gray, green, white, and possibly pink, and is expected to launch at $799.99 — a $100 increase over last year’s model.

Article Name: DJI Clears Out Mic 3 Wireless Microphone Bundle at Record Low With Charging Case Included, Ready for the Osmo Ecosystem
Article Text:
The built-in microphones of smartphones and cameras can get the job done, but they don’t really offer that professional-level, clear, crisp audio quality. That’s where a dedicated mic can come in handy. Now is a great time to pick a few up, as Amazon just dropped the DJI mic 3 bundle by 21%.

This bundle usually goes for $329, but the current discount lets you grab it for $259 today. Shipping is free for all shoppers, so this is all you’ve got to pay today. This bundle comes with two DJI Mic 3 TX, one RX, a charging case, a Mic 3 mobile phone adapter (USB-C), and more. Getting two microphones means you can film adventures with a friend, or record interviews without having to pass a single mic back and forth. It’s also great for longer solo sessions, as you can simply switch to the second microphone when one runs out of battery.

The DJI Mic 3 TX is lightweight and compact. It clips easily onto your shirt and can be rotated to adjust the angle for optimal audio. It even looks aesthetically pleasing on camera, so you won’t really have to worry about it feeling awkward or appearing bulky. Once the transmitter is on and the receiver is connected to your phone or camera, you can choose between three voice styles and two noise cancellation modes. Voice styles include Regular for balanced audio, Rich for extra emphasis on the lows, and Bright to give your voice some extra clarity. The two noise cancellation modes, on the other hand, are Indoor and Outdoor. The former is ideal for already-quieter settings that don’t have a lot of background noise to cancel. It reduces fan and AC noises while keeping audio clear. The outdoor noise-cancelling mode is much stronger and significantly reduces ambient noise in crowded spaces.

What’s more, this wireless microphone supports Dual-band anti-interference for flawless, lossless audio transmission. This means you can walk around freely without any signal cuts. As for compatibility, the setup works nicely with a range of devices, but the transmitter can connect to Osmo Mobile 8, Osmo Action 6, Osmo Nano, Osmo 360, Osmo Mobile 7P, Osmo Action 5 Pro, Osmo Action 4, or Osmo Pocket 3 even without a receiver!

The transmitter lasts around 8 hours on a single charge, while a receiver offers up to 10 hours of usage in one go. A fully charged charging case can easily support up to 28 hours of filming.

You can now snap up the DJI mic 3 bundle for $259, down from $329. Just note that this 21% discount won’t last long, so it’s best to grab it as soon as you can.
GPT Summary: The article discusses a current discount on the DJI Mic 3 wireless microphone bundle, which is now available for $259 on Amazon, down from its usual price of $329, with free shipping included. This bundle is ideal for enhancing audio quality over standard smartphone and camera microphones, particularly for professional-level clarity. It includes two DJI Mic 3 transmitters, a receiver, a charging case, and a mobile phone adapter, among other components, making it suitable for both solo and dual-recording scenarios. Users benefit from features like three voice styles and two noise cancellation modes, tailored for various environments, and enjoy dual-band anti-interference for seamless, lossless audio transmission. The transmitters are compact, have an aesthetically pleasing design, and last about 8 hours per charge, while the charging case provides up to 28 hours of support. The setup is compatible with various devices, especially within the Osmo Ecosystem, even without a receiver. The article advises acting quickly to take advantage of this limited-time 21% discount offer.
Gemini Summary: Amazon is currently offering a significant 21% discount on the DJI Mic 3 wireless microphone bundle, dropping its price from $329 to $259 with free shipping. This comprehensive bundle includes two DJI Mic 3 TX transmitters, one RX receiver, a charging case, and a mobile phone adapter, making it ideal for professional-level audio recording for interviews, vlogs, or solo sessions. The compact and lightweight microphones offer three voice styles and two noise cancellation modes (Indoor and Outdoor) for optimal sound quality in various environments, alongside dual-band anti-interference technology for lossless transmission. With impressive battery life—8 hours for transmitters, 10 for the receiver, and up to 28 hours with the charging case—and direct compatibility with many Osmo devices, this deal provides an excellent opportunity to upgrade audio quality before the limited-time discount expires.

Article Name: Here's your first look at Google's upcoming 'Proactive Assistance' feature for Gemini
Article Text: Google is steadily expanding Gemini’s role across your phone, with a clear push toward making it more context-aware and deeply integrated into your daily usage. Earlier this year, the company rolled out Personal Intelligence, a feature that taps into apps like Gmail, Photos, YouTube, and Search to deliver more relevant suggestions and tailored responses. Now, it looks like Google is working on taking things a step further with a new “Proactive Assistance” feature in Gemini that can surface helpful suggestions based on what you’re doing, even before you think to ask. We spotted Proactive Assistance while digging through the Google app (version 17.18.22.sa.arm64), where we also managed to enable the feature. While not yet publicly available, the screenshots we managed to grab give us a clear idea of how Proactive Assistance will work once Google rolls it out. Proactive Assistance lives inside the Gemini settings menu, where users can toggle it on or off and control which apps feed into it. From what we’ve seen so far, you can turn the feature on/off with a single switch. According to the in-app description, Proactive Assistance pulls from three main sources, including what’s currently on your screen, your notifications, and the data from apps you explicitly allow. The goal seems to be to deliver timely, relevant suggestions based on what you’re doing. These could include things like reminders, contextual actions, or quick insights that appear right when they’re useful, not after you go looking for them. A Connected apps section lets you decide which data sources Gemini can use for the feature. Currently, the supported apps include Contacts and Messages. However, additional integrations like Gmail and Calendar fall under “Personal Intelligence apps,” which links to the broader connected apps dashboard. Google is also keeping user privacy in mind and notes that all data used by the Proactive Assistance feature is processed entirely on-device, with information stored in a private, encrypted environment. There’s no word yet on when Google plans to roll this out publicly, but given how fleshed out the feature already is, it may not be far off.
GPT Summary: Google is enhancing Gemini's capabilities on smartphones by introducing a new "Proactive Assistance" feature aimed at providing context-aware suggestions tailored to user activities. This feature extends the functionality of the previously launched Personal Intelligence by offering real-time, helpful insights, reminders, and actions based on what's currently happening on the device, such as visible content, notifications, and user-defined app data. Although not yet publicly released, Proactive Assistance can be toggled on or off in the Gemini settings, and users have control over which apps contribute to its insights. The current iteration supports Contacts and Messages, with a broader integration plan for Gmail and Calendar under the Personal Intelligence suite. Importantly, Google emphasizes user privacy, ensuring that all data processing occurs on-device in a secure environment. While an official release date is not provided, the developed state of the feature suggests a public rollout could be imminent.
Gemini Summary: Google is developing "Proactive Assistance," a new feature for Gemini designed to offer timely and context-aware suggestions before users even think to ask. Building on the existing Personal Intelligence, this feature will leverage information from what's on screen, notifications, and explicitly allowed apps like Contacts and Messages, with broader integrations from Gmail and Calendar via Personal Intelligence. Users will have control over enabling or disabling the feature and managing which apps provide data, with Google emphasizing on-device processing and encrypted storage for privacy. While not yet publicly released, the feature appears well-developed and could be rolling out soon.
Grok Summary:
**Summary:**

Google is developing a new “Proactive Assistance” feature for Gemini that aims to deliver timely, context-aware suggestions without requiring the user to ask first. The feature, discovered in the latest version of the Google app, analyzes what’s currently on your screen, your notifications, and data from apps you explicitly allow (such as Contacts, Messages, and through Personal Intelligence, Gmail and Calendar). Users will be able to toggle it on or off directly in Gemini’s settings and choose which apps can feed it information. Google emphasizes privacy, stating that all processing happens entirely on-device in a private, encrypted environment. While the feature is not yet publicly available, it appears largely complete and could be rolled out soon.
